coredata.dc:description: Uric acid (UA) is a crucial biomarker for various metabolic and renal disorders, making its accurate determination essential for clinical diagnostics and disease management. This study presents a novel fluorometric method for UA detection using a glutathione@cadmium/carbon dots/uricase system (GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system), which offers enhanced sensitivity and selectivity compared to existing techniques. The method employs a cascade reaction mechanism initiated by uricase-catalyzed oxidation of UA to produce H2O2. This H2O2 subsequently oxidizes GSH to oxidized glutathione (GSSG), releasing cadmium ions from GSH complexes. The liberated Cd2+ ions interact with two types of carbon dots (CDs) in the system: blue-emitting CDs (BCDs) and red-emitting CDs (RCDs). This interaction triggers a dual fluorescence response, characterized by a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 400 nm (BCDs) and an increase at 610 nm (RCDs), enabling ratiometric detection of UA levels. Comprehensive characterization and mechanistic investigations were conducted using various optical and morphological techniques, including transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The method exhibits outstanding analytical performance, characterized by strong linearity (R2 = 0.9932), a broad detection range (0.05–7.0 μM), and a low detection limit of 0.015 μM. The method demonstrated excellent selectivity against common interfering substances and was successfully applied to human serum and urine samples with high recovery values (97.65 % to 99.20 %), highlighting its potential for clinical applications. This innovative approach combines the specificity of enzymatic recognition with the sensitivity of dual-emission fluorescence, offering a promising tool for accurate UA quantification in complex biological matrices.

row.entry: Mechanism
Ref.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 → •OH → Fe(II) to Fe(III) Oxidation → Fluorescence Quenching of A-CDs.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + CQDs → OPD Oxidation → DAP Formation → Increase Fluorescence at 550 nm of DAP, Decrease of CQDs at 450 nm (Inner Filter Effect of DAP).
UA + CDs → Ground-State Complex Formation → CDs Aggregation → Fluorescence Quenching
UA + Uricase → H2O2 → •OH → Fe(II) to Fe(III) Oxidation → S, N-CDs Fluorescence Quenching.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 → CdS QDs Quenching.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + G-Quadruplex/Hemin DNAzyme → H2O2 Decomposition → •OH → Caffeic Acid Oxidation to Corresponding Quinone → GQDs Fluorescence Quenching.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + CdTe NPs → Fluorescence Quenching
S,N-CDs + MnO2 Nanosheets → Fluorescence Quenching → UA Addition → MnO2 Reduction to Mn2+ → Partial Fluorescence Recovery.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 → H2O2-Driven PVP-AuNPs → Fluorescence Quenching CS-AuNCs
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + BSA-Protected AuNCs → Au-S Bond Breakage → Fluorescence Quenching
H2O2 + Co2+ → Co3+ + Hydroxyl Radicals → AUR Oxidation → Fluorescent Product → UA Inhibits Co2+ Oxidation → Fluorescence Decrease.
UA + Porous g-C3N4 → Surface State Change → Fluorescence Alteration
UA + Uricase → H2O2/Fe2+ + Protein-Stabilized AuNCs → Fluorescence Quenching at 615 nm + Coumarin Oxidation → Increased Fluorescence at 450 nm
N-CQDs + AgTNPs → Fluorescence Reduction of N-CQDs → UA Prevents AgTNPs Etching → Fluorescence Alteration.
g-CQDs + UA → Fluorescence Quenching → Static Quenching
UA + APBA-CDs → Complex Formation with Boric Acid → Fluorescence Quenching.
Arg-CD + UA → Electrostatic Interaction + Hydrogen Bond Formation → Dual-Emission Fluorescence Activation
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + I− → •OH Radicals → OPD Oxidation to DAP → N-CDs Fluorescence Quenching.
MPA-Capped ZnS/ZnS QDs + UA → Fluorescence Intensity Increase.
CDs + MnO2 Nanosheets → FRET Quenching of CDs → UA Introduced → MnO2 Decomposition to Mn2+ → Fluorescence Recovery.
AgNPs + N-CQDs → Fluorescence Quenching of N-CQDs + H2O2 released from UA → Ag+ Formation → Fluorescence Enhancement.
Ag-CQDs + UA → Bond Formation → Electron/Energy Transfer → Fluorescence Quenching.
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + I− → •OH Radicals → OPD Oxidation to DAP → Fluorescence Quenching of N, Co-CDs.
CDs@Cr-MOF + UA → Fluorescence Quenching.
Cu2+@MIL-91(Al) + UA → Luminescence Rebound of Eu3+
UA + Uricase → H2O2 + GSH@Cd → GSSG + Cd2+ → Cd2+ Increase Fluorescence of RCDs and Decrease fluorescence of BCDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uric acid (UA) is a crucial biomarker for various metabolic and renal disorders, making its accurate determination essential for clinical diagnostics and disease management. This study presents a novel fluorometric method for UA detection using a glutathione@cadmium/carbon dots/uricase system (GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system), which offers enhanced sensitivity and selectivity compared to existing techniques. The method employs a cascade reaction mechanism initiated by uricase-catalyzed oxidation of UA to produce H2O2. This H2O2 subsequently oxidizes GSH to oxidized glutathione (GSSG), releasing cadmium ions from GSH complexes. The liberated Cd2+ ions interact with two types of carbon dots (CDs) in the system: blue-emitting CDs (BCDs) and red-emitting CDs (RCDs). This interaction triggers a dual fluorescence response, characterized by a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 400 nm (BCDs) and an increase at 610 nm (RCDs), enabling ratiometric detection of UA levels. Comprehensive characterization and mechanistic investigations were conducted using various optical and morphological techniques, including transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The method exhibits outstanding analytical performance, characterized by strong linearity (R2 = 0.9932), a broad detection range (0.05–7.0 μM), and a low detection limit of 0.015 μM. The method demonstrated excellent selectivity against common interfering substances and was successfully applied to human serum and urine samples with high recovery values (97.65 % to 99.20 %), highlighting its potential for clinical applications. This innovative approach combines the specificity of enzymatic recognition with the sensitivity of dual-emission fluorescence, offering a promising tool for accurate UA quantification in complex biological matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is a heterocyclic compound produced as the product of purine metabolism in humans. UA plays important roles in the body, acting as an antioxidant and contributing to neuroprotection [1]. However, its most significant function is as a metabolic waste product that needs to be excreted. Normal serum UA levels typically range from 150 to 400 μM, while normal urine levels are generally between 1500 to 4500 μM [2]. Elevated serum UA levels (hyperuricemia) can indicate conditions such as gout, kidney stones, or metabolic disorders, while very low levels might suggest liver or kidney dysfunction. Reduced urinary UA excretion could indicate kidney problems or lead to UA stone formation [3]. Conversely, excessive urinary UA might point to overproduction or increased cell turnover, as seen in some cancers or with certain medications [4]. Accurate determination of UA levels is crucial for diagnosing and monitoring various metabolic and renal disorders, assessing the risk of gout and kidney stones, and guiding treatment decisions in conditions where UA plays a role.

Various methods have been previously reported for UA detection, each with its own set of advantages and limitations. Chemiluminescence offers high sensitivity but can suffer from interference from other biological molecules and requires specialized equipment [5,6]. Colorimetric methods are simple and cost-effective but often lack the sensitivity needed for low concentrations and can be affected by sample color [7,8]. Electrochemical techniques provide good sensitivity and selectivity, but electrode fouling and interference from other electroactive species can be problematic [9]. Chromatographic methods like GC and HPLC offer excellent separation and quantification capabilities but are time-consuming, require expensive equipment, and often involve complex sample preparation [10–12]. Additionally, many of these methods face challenges with direct application to complex biological matrices, often requiring sample pretreatment or extraction steps. The limitations of these techniques have driven the continuous search for improved methods that combine high sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity, and applicability to real-world samples for UA detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising new advance in the detection of drugs and biological molecules [13,14]. These nanomaterials offer several advantages that address the limitations of traditional detection methods [15–17]. CDs are known for their excellent optical properties, including high photostability, tunable fluorescence, and resistance to photobleaching [18,19]. They can be easily synthesized from a variety of carbon sources using simple, cost-effective methods, making them an attractive alternative to more expensive reagents [20,21].

Despite the promise of carbon dots (CDs) for UA detection, many CD-based methods still face limitations in sensitivity, selectivity, and practicality. Some require complex preparation steps, expensive precursors, or suffer from interference in biological samples. Issues with reproducibility, stability, and the need for specialized equipment persist. These challenges underscore the need for developing improved methods that combine simple, cost-effective CD synthesis with straightforward detection protocols, while achieving high sensitivity and selectivity for UA in complex biological samples.

This novel fluorometric method for UA detection leverages a sophisticated glutathione@cadmium/carbon dots/uricase system (GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system), offering a unique approach to overcome limitations of previous techniques. Our method introduces a unique signal amplification cascade that enhances sensitivity without complex instrumentation. The method ingeniously combines enzymatic specificity with the sensitive optical properties of BCDs and RCDs in a ratiometric detection scheme. By utilizing uricase to generate H2O2 from UA, the system initiates a cascade of reactions: H2O2 oxidizes GSH to oxidized glutathione (GSSG), releasing cadmium ions from their GSH complex. These liberated Cd2+ ions then interact with the CDs, causing a dual fluorescence response. The fluorescence of BCDs decreases at 400 nm, while the fluorescence of RCDs increases at 610 nm. This ratiometric measurement enhances the method's reliability by minimizing environmental interferences and instrument fluctuations. The use of CDs provides excellent optical properties and potential biocompatibility, while the enzymatic step ensures high specificity for UA. The method's success in analyzing human serum and urine samples demonstrates its practical applicability in complex biological matrices, addressing a key challenge in UA detection. This innovative approach combines sensitivity, selectivity, and real-world applicability, representing a significant advancement in the field of UA quantification and potentially opening new avenues for biomarker detection in clinical diagnostics. Furthermore, our system's potential for multiplexed detection by using different enzyme-CD pairs for various analytes sets it apart from many existing single-analyte methods.

H2O2, UA, and uricase were procured from Sigma − Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). O-phenylenediamine (OPD), alanine, allantoin, ascorbic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), cholesterol, ethylenediamine, cysteine, dopamine, semicarbazide, fructose, glucose, glutathione, glycine, sulfosalicylic acid, lactose, L-proline, sucrose, threonine, tryptophan, cadmium perchlorate hexahydrate, and urea were purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Ammonium chloride, calcium chloride, copper sulphate, ferric chloride, magnesium chloride, cadmium chloride, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, zinc sulphate, and sodium phosphate were supplied by alpha chemika Co., India. The double-distilled water used in all experiments had a resistivity around 18 MΩ.cm−1.

The study employed a range of advanced analytical techniques to characterize the materials and assess their properties. A JEM-1400 instrument was used to capture transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, providing insights into the nanoscale structure. Spectroscopic analyses were conducted using a Shimazu 1601 PC spectrophotometer for UV–visible spectra and a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer for fluorescence measurements. To investigate molecular bonding, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. The chemical composition and electronic states of the used CDs were examined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Surface charge characteristics were evaluated by measuring zeta potentials with a Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Raman and FTIR spectra were recorded using a Bruker MultiRAM FT-Raman spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Billerica, MA, USA).

The synthesis of both RCDs and BCDs was accomplished using a reported hydrothermal method [22], with distinct precursors for each type. For RCDs, 0.5 g of semicarbazide and 1.0 g of OPD were used, while BCDs employed 0.2 g of sulfosalicylic acid and 0.2 mL of ethylenediamine. In both cases, the precursors were dispersed in 50 mL of double distilled water (DDW), transferred to Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclaves, and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solutions underwent centrifugation (8000 rpm for 15 min for RCDs, 10 min for BCDs). The supernatants were then filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filters and dialyzed using 1000 Da dialysis bags for 24 h. Finally, both dialyzed dispersions were freeze-dried to obtain powdered products, which were stored at 4 °C for preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield of the prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum yield calculations can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

The preparation of the GSH@Cd complex followed an established protocol [23], with fresh solutions made before each analysis. The process began by dissolving GSH in deoxygenated water containing 10 % D2O, yielding a solution with a pH between 2.5 and 2.7. Subsequently, precise amounts of Cd(ClO4)2·6H2O were added under constant stirring. The final step involved the dropwise addition of 2 M sodium hydroxide solution to adjust the pH. Notably, a white precipitate formed at pH 3.0, which completely dissolved upon reaching pH 7.0.

Initially, a mixture is prepared in a 10.0 mL volumetric flask by combining 500 μL of BCDs, 500 μL of RCDs, and 1000 μL of GSH@Cd. Concurrently, in a separate flask, the enzymatic reaction between UAand uricase is performed by mixing 500 μL of UA (at various concentrations) with 500 μL of uricase enzyme at pH 8.5 (BR buffer) and 35 °C for 15 min, then diluting to 10.0 mL with double distilled water. The detection assay is then performed by combining 1.0 mL from each of these prepared solutions in a new flask, incubating for 5.0 min, and diluting to the mark with BR buffer (pH 7.0). A blank solution is prepared similarly, omitting the UA. Finally, the emission spectrum is recorded after excitation at 280 nm.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system, we collected two human serum and urine samples from Assiut University Hospital. Before initiating the experiment, the samples were pretreated to eliminate impurities such as suspended particles and proteins. Serum samples were centrifuged at 5,000 rpm for 15 min, while urine samples were processed at 8,000 rpm for the same duration. After centrifugation, the supernatant from each sample was extracted and diluted with double distilled water, using a 10-fold dilution for serum and a 50-fold dilution for urine. For the final analysis, 1000 μL of each diluted supernatant and spiked samples (prepared by standard UA addition) were introduced into the detection system. Each experiment and measurement were conducted in triplicate (n = 3), following the procedure detailed in section 2.6.

A comprehensive characterization of the CDs was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM imaging revealed that both BCDs (Fig. S1A) and RCDs (Fig. S1B) are well-dispersed and nearly spherical, with BCDs ranging from 2.0 to 6.0 nm in diameter (mean size 3.4 nm) and RCDs from 2.0 to 6.0 nm (mean size 3.3 nm). XRD analysis showed broad peaks at 20.65° for BCDs (Fig. S1C) and 20.19° for RCDs (Fig. S1D), indicating a partially amorphous carbon structure with the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon domains [24]. The absence of sharp peaks in the XRD data further confirms the lack of large crystalline impurities or highly ordered graphitic structures in both types of CDs [22].

FT-IR analysis of both BCDs (Fig. 1 A) and RCDs (Fig. 1B) reveals distinctive spectral features indicative of their surface functionalities. BCDs exhibit two broad peaks at 3442 cm−1 and 3047 cm−1, likely corresponding to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively [25]. The peaks at 1645 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 can be attributed to C = O and C = C stretching, while those at 1478 cm−1, 1159 cm−1, and 1036 cm−1 may represent C-N, C-O, and C-O-C stretching vibrations. The lower wavenumber peaks (878 cm−1, 678 cm−1, and 593 cm−1) could be associated with various bending modes. In contrast, RCDs show two distinct peaks at 3135 cm−1 and 3018 cm−1, possibly indicating N-H and aromatic C-H stretching. The peak at 2806 cm−1 might represent aliphatic C-H stretching, while 1741 cm−1 suggests C = O stretching. The peaks at 1485 cm−1, 1397 cm−1, 1200 cm−1, and 1091 cm−1 could be attributed to various C-N, C-O, and C-C vibrations [26]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to determine the elemental composition of both BCDs and RCDs. The XPS spectrum analysis showed that RCDs consisted of C, N, and O, as illustrated in Fig. 1C. In contrast, the BCDs probe was found to contain C, N, O, and S, as shown in Fig. 1D [27].

The spectroscopic analysis of both RCDs and BCDs reveals distinct optical characteristics. RCDs show a UV–Vis absorption spectrum with a shoulder peak at 222 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions, and a sharp peak at 280 nm, indicative of n-π* transitions (Fig. 2 A) [16]. A broad absorption band centered at 415 nm suggests the presence of extended conjugated systems or larger aromatic domains. RCDs exhibit a maximum emission at 610 nm when excited at 280 nm, with this emission remaining consistent across excitation wavelengths from 250 to 300 nm (Fig. S2A), indicating a uniform emissive state or narrow distribution of emissive sites [28]. In contrast, BCDs display a UV–Vis absorption spectrum with a shoulder peak at 235 nm (π-π* transitions) and a sharp peak at 298 nm, likely due to n-π* transitions of carbonyl groups, imine groups, or heteroatom-containing aromatic structures (Fig. 2B). BCDs demonstrate a maximum emission at 400 nm when excited at 298 nm, with this emission remaining stable across excitation wavelengths from 250 to 340 nm (Fig. S2B). This excitation-independent emission behavior in both RCDs and BCDs suggests efficient energy transfer and radiative recombination processes within the CDs, as well as the presence of well-defined emissive states or narrow distributions of emissive sites in both types of CDs [28]. Although 280 nm is the optimal excitation wavelength for only one of the two CDs, it was selected to enable the excitation of both types. However, this wavelength results in a slightly reduced fluorescence emission for BCDs. The quantum yield of RCDs is 12.3 %, whereas that of BCDs is 16.5 %.

The coordination of the thiol group in the GSH@Cd complex is evidenced by a significant change in the Raman spectrum. In free GSH, a distinct band at 2,525 cm−1 is typically observed, which corresponds to the ν(S–H) stretching vibration of the cysteinyl thiol group. However, this band is notably absent in the spectrum of the GSH@Cd complex. The disappearance of the ν(S–H) band strongly indicates that the SH group is no longer present in its free form (Fig. S2). This spectral change provides compelling evidence that the sulfur atom of the cysteinyl thiol group is directly coordinated to the Cd2+ ion in the complex, confirming the formation of a metal-sulfur bond and the successful synthesis of the GSH@Cd coordination compound.

A comprehensive investigation was carried out to assess the fluorescence stability of BCDs and RCDs under different environmental conditions. The fluorescence response to pH changes was examined within a range of 2.0 to 10.0, where both types of CDs showed peak fluorescence intensity around pH 7.0 (Figs. S4A and S4B). A significant decline in photoluminescence was detected in strongly acidic environments, likely due to the protonation or deprotonation of surface functional groups, which in turn affected the electronic structure and fluorescence properties of the dots [29]. The effect of ionic strength on fluorescence stability was tested by exposing the CDs to NaCl concentrations from 0.01 to 3.0 M (Figs. S4C and D). Both BCDs and RCDs maintained stable photoluminescence intensity across this concentration range, demonstrating their durability in solutions with varying ionic strengths. Photostability was evaluated by subjecting the CDs to prolonged UV irradiation for up to 5.0 h (Figs. S4E and S3F). Both BCDs and RCDs exhibited strong resistance to photobleaching, with minimal reduction in fluorescence intensity observed throughout the exposure period. These extensive stability tests emphasize the resilience of the synthesized CDs under a variety of conditions, including pH variations, changes in ionic strength, and extended UV exposure. The robust fluorescence performance of BCDs and RCDs indicates their strong potential for use in a wide range of applications where reliable fluorescent probes are essential. Additionally, the results from the batch-by-batch experiments (Fig. S5) showed negligible variation in fluorescence intensity, suggesting that the biosensor demonstrates outstanding reproducibility and stability.

Optimization of reaction conditions is crucial for enhancing the sensitivity of the proposed sensor. Previous research has established that uricase catalyzes UA most effectively at pH 8.5 and 35 °C with a 15-minute incubation period [30]. To maximize the efficiency of UA quantification in biological samples, we focused on optimizing three key parameters: pH, incubation time, and GSH@Cd volume. We used the fluorescence intensity ratio F610/F400 (emission at 610 nm / emission at 400 nm) as a metric to evaluate the signal response. The impact of pH was investigated over a range of 5.0 to 10.0. As illustrated in Fig. S6A, the F610/F400 ratio initially increased with pH, reached a maximum at pH 7.0, and then decreased. Consequently, pH 7.0 was selected for subsequent experiments. The observed decrease in the fluorescence ratio at higher pH values may be attributed to the precipitation of Cd2+ ions under alkaline conditions. The effect of incubation time on the signal response was also examined (Fig. S6B). The F610/F400 ratio increased with longer incubation times, stabilizing after approximately 5.0 min. To ensure a stable signal and maximum fluorescence ratio, we adopted a 5.0-minute incubation time for further studies. Finally, we investigated the influence of GSH@Cd volume on the sensor response (Fig. S6C). The F610/F400 ratio showed a gradual increase with increasing GSH@Cd volume, plateauing at 1.0 mL. These optimized conditions − pH 7.0, 5.0-minute incubation time, and 1.0 mL GSH@Cd volume were employed in subsequent experiments to achieve the most sensitive and reliable UA detection in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UA detection assay using the GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system for UA detection was evaluated under optimized conditions. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at various UA concentrations. As the UA concentration increased from 0 to 7.0 μM, a concurrent decrease in fluorescence intensity at 400 nm and increase at 610 nm were observed (Fig. 3 A). The ratio of fluorescence intensities (F610/F400) exhibited a linear relationship with UA concentration in the range of 0.05 – 7.0 μM (Fig. 3B). The linear regression equation was determined to be F610/F400 = 0.36 + 0.15C(UA), with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9938. Here, F400 and F610 represent the fluorescence intensities at 400 nm and 610 nm, respectively. The detection limit for UA was calculated to be 0.015 μM, based on the formula 3δ/k, where δ represents the standard error of the estimate (n = 3), and k is the slope of the linear regression line.

In human plasma, normal serum UA levels typically range from 150 to 400 μM, while normal urine levels are generally between 1500 to 4500 μM. Our method's linear range (0.05 – 7.0 μM) encompasses this entire physiological range after several dilutions, allowing for accurate quantification in normal samples. Decreased UA levels (<150 μM in serum) have been associated with various pathological conditions, including certain neurodegenerative disorders and impaired antioxidant capacity [2]. Our detection limit (0.014 μM) is well below this clinical threshold, enabling the identification of potentially pathological states. In certain pathological conditions, such as gout or kidney stones, serum UA levels may be elevated to 500–800 μM [31]. Our method's upper linear range (7.0 μM) accommodates these elevated levels after appropriate dilution, making it suitable for monitoring UA-related disorders and their treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with previous fluorometric methods
ce:section.ce:para: Given the numerous fluorometric methods available for uric acid detection, a comprehensive comparison was conducted between the proposed method and other reported approaches (Table 1 and Table S1). The proposed method for UA detection represents a significant advancement over previously reported techniques, offering a unique combination of sensitivity, selectivity, and practicality. This innovative design addresses several limitations observed in earlier methods. The proposed dual-signal amplification contributes to the method's superior sensitivity compared to many single-signal approaches as shown in Table S1. The use of uricase ensures high specificity for UA, overcoming selectivity issues seen in direct interaction methods [32–35]. This enzymatic approach minimizes interference from other biomolecules commonly found in biological samples. Unlike many previous methods that rely on single-wavelength measurements, this technique employs a ratiometric approach (F610/F400). This ratiometric approach improves the method's robustness by minimizing the impact of environmental changes, sample matrix effects, and instrument variations, resulting in more reliable and reproducible outcomes. The use of CDs and a simple metal–ligand complex (GSH@Cd) offers greater stability compared to methods utilizing unstable nanoparticles, complex DNA structures, or protein-stabilized nanoclusters [36–39]. This stability translates to improved shelf-life and consistent performance over time. Unlike methods employing toxic metals [40–44], this approach uses more benign materials, aligning with green chemistry principles and reducing potential environmental and health risks. The method's design allows for potential application in various biological matrices (serum, urine) without significant interference potentially due to sample dilution, a challenge for some previous methods [45–47]. The synthesis of CDs and the use of common biochemicals (GSH, Cd2+) offer a potentially more cost-effective solution compared to methods requiring expensive materials or complex nanostructures [44,48]. Unlike methods that rely solely on fluorescence quenching or enhancement, this approach provides a dual-signal change, offering an additional layer of confidence in the detection process and potentially reducing false positives.

The proposed fluorometric method for UA detection employs a GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system, which operates through a cascade of reactions. Initially, uricase catalyzes the conversion of UA to produce H2O2. This H2O2 then oxidizes GSH to its disulfide form (GSSG), resulting in the release of Cd2+ ions from their GSH complexes. The liberated Cd2+ ions subsequently interact with two types of CDs present in the system: BCDs and RCDs. This interaction triggers a dual fluorescence response, characterized by a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 400 nm (associated with BCDs) and an increase at 610 nm (associated with RCDs), thereby enabling sensitive and specific detection of UA levels. The underlying mechanisms responsible for these contrasting effects will be elucidated in the following paragraphs.

The quenching mechanism of the BCDs in the presence of Cd2+ was investigated through various spectroscopic and analytical techniques. Before studying the quenching mechanism, we ensured that BCDs were not influenced by the presence of H2O2, UA, uricase, or GSH (Fig. 4 A). Fig. S7A demonstrates that the absorption spectrum of Cd2+ does not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of BCDs, ruling out the inner filter effect (IFE) as the quenching mechanism. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted to further elucidate the quenching process. As illustrated in Fig. S7B, the fluorescence decay curves of BCDs is shown before and after the addition of Cd2+. The fluorescence lifetime of BCDs remained unchanged after the addition of Cd2+ suggesting a static quenching process. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy revealed a red shift and reduction in the intensity of the BCDs' absorption peak at 298 nm upon addition of Cd2+ (Fig. 4B). This observation suggests the formation of a new complex, likely due to chelation between the functional groups of BCDs and Cd2+ ions. The oxygen atoms in –COOH, –OH, and SO3H groups, as well as the nitrogen atom in –NH2 groups of BCDs, are thought to coordinate with Cd2+ ions by donating lone pair electrons, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Zeta potential measurements provided additional insights into the detection mechanism. The initial large negative potential of BCDs (−25.11 mV), attributed to carboxyl and O-functional groups, shifted to a positive value (6.02 mV) upon Cd2+ addition (Fig. S7C). This change confirms the interaction between BCDs and Cd2+ ions through ligand-to-metal charge transfer. The combined results from UV–vis absorption spectra and zeta potential measurements exhibit characteristics consistent with a static quenching mechanism.

When Cd2+ ions were added to RCDs, fluorescence enhancement occurred, while no change in fluorescence intensity was observed after the addition of H2O2, UA, uricase, or GSH (Fig. 4C). Two primary fluorescence enhancement mechanisms are generally recognized: metal surface enhanced fluorescence (MEF) or surface plasmon enhanced fluorescence (SPEF), and chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) [56–58]. The latter involves the formation of complexes between surface functional groups and metal ions. Recent studies suggest that chelation between surface functional groups and metal ions can reduce internal charge transfer (ICT), enhance photoinduced electron transfer, and consequently increase emission intensity. In our study, the observed fluorescence enhancement likely results from the diminished electron donor capacity of amino groups when RCDs chelate with Cd2+. This process may suppress ICT transitions while promoting photoinduced electron transfer to the RCDs/Cd2+ complex [58]. The absorption spectra of RCDs (Fig. 5 D) showed no significant changes after adding Cd2+ ions, effectively ruling out the SPEF mechanism. Additionally, zeta potential measurements showed an increase from −10.2 eV to 5.2 eV upon Cd2+ addition (Fig. S7D), attributed to electrostatic neutralization.

Considering the structure of the surface ligands, the interaction between Cd2+ ions and amide/amine groups likely forms a polyatomic ring structure, creating a chelation complex. This process aligns with the CHEF mechanism [59]. The resulting larger conjugate plane and increased rigidity lead to a more stable structure, which in turn enhances luminous efficiency. Additionally, this extended conjugated system facilitates the excitation of π electrons, promoting fluorescence emission. The observed enhancement in fluorescence intensity in the presence of Cd2+ can be primarily attributed to the coordination of Cd2+ with oxygen and nitrogen atoms, forming a rigid chelation complex [60].

Despite uric acid's reported absorption maximum at approximately 290 nm, no spectral overlap was observed in our detection method. This absence of interference can be attributed to the fact that the upper limit of uric acid concentrations in our assay falls well below the detection threshold of conventional UV–visible spectrophotometric methods.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the proposed UA detection method in biological samples, the system's response to potential interfering substances commonly found in human fluids was investigated. A comprehensive panel of potential interferents present in human biological fluids was selected, including amino acids (alanine, cysteine, glycine, L-proline, threonine, tryptophan), biomolecules (creatinine, allantoin, ascorbic acid, cholesterol, dopamine, urea, oxalic acid, tartaric acid, and hippuric acid), proteins (bovine serum albumin), carbohydrates (fructose, glucose, lactose, sucrose), antioxidants (glutathione), and various ions (NH4 +, Ca2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Cl−, Br−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−) (Fig. 5A and B). The UA concentration was maintained at 5.0 μM, while the potential interfering substances were tested at a tenfold higher concentration of 50.0 μM. Remarkably, only UA induced a significant change in the fluorescence intensity ratio (F610/F400). This high selectivity can be attributed to the specificity of the uricase enzyme, which exclusively catalyzes the oxidation of UA to generate H2O2. Additionally, the dilution step during sample pretreatment helps reduce interference from potential interferants. The negligible interference from other substances underscores the method's robustness and reliability for UA detection in complex biological matrices. These findings demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits excellent selectivity and dependability for UA quantification in the presence of various potentially interfering compounds typically found in biological samples.

To evaluate intra-day precision, three replicate analyses were conducted at three different concentration levels (1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 µM) on a single day. Inter-day precision was similarly measured by testing the same concentration levels over three consecutive days. The standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviation (RSD) values obtained from these tests are displayed in Table S2. RSD values under 3 % indicate exceptional precision, confirming the method's reliability, stability and reproducibility (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the method's practical applicability, UA levels were measured in human serum and urine samples. The results, presented in Table 2 , demonstrate the method's effectiveness in real biological matrices. For serum samples, the recovery rates ranged from 97.80 % to 99.20 %, while urine samples showed recoveries between 97.65 % and 98.20 %. Both sample types exhibited relative standard deviations (RSD) below 3.09 %, indicating good precision. To validate the method's accuracy, a comparative analysis was conducted using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) on the same samples [61]. As shown in Table 2, there is no significant difference in the percentage recovery between the two methods. These findings indicate that the newly developed method for UA determination in urine samples is comparable to the established HPLC technique, demonstrating good agreement between the two approaches. This validation underscores the reliability and potential of the proposed method for practical applications in clinical settings.

This study presents a novel and highly effective fluorometric method for UA detection, leveraging a unique GSH@Cd/CDs/uricase system. The developed approach demonstrates several significant advantages over existing techniques, including enhanced sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and robust performance in complex biological matrices. The innovative cascade reaction mechanism, initiated by uricase-catalyzed oxidation of UA, coupled with the dual-emission properties of blue and RCDs, provides a sensitive and reliable ratiometric detection system. This design effectively addresses common challenges in UA sensing, such as interference from other biomolecules and environmental factors. Comprehensive characterization using various analytical techniques confirmed the structural and optical properties of the CDs, providing insights into the underlying detection mechanism. The method's high selectivity against potential interferents commonly found in biological samples further underscores its practicality for clinical applications. Notably, the successful application of this method to human serum and urine samples, yielding excellent recovery values, demonstrates its potential for real-world diagnostic use. The ability to accurately quantify UA levels in these complex matrices highlights the robustness and reliability of the developed sensor. Furthermore, the use of relatively benign materials and straightforward synthesis procedures aligns with green chemistry principles, making this approach environmentally friendly and potentially cost-effective for large-scale implementation.
